section.title: Create in Me a Clean Heart
section.sectionRationale: David's great penitential psalm, written after Nathan the prophet confronted him over his adultery with Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah. It is the model of biblical repentance: honest confession, a plea not merely for pardon but for a new heart, and a resolve to praise and teach others, anticipating the new heart promised in the new covenant.
section.thesis: David's honest confession after his sin with Bathsheba models true repentance, casting himself wholly on God's mercy and pleading not just for pardon but for a new heart, a prayer fully answered in the new covenant and the Spirit poured out through Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the plea for mercy and confession of sin reaching back to birth; the central prayer for a clean heart and a right spirit; the vow to teach transgressors and offer a broken and contrite heart; and a closing prayer for the good of Zion.

units.label: Have mercy on me, O God
units.note: the plea and confession of inbred sin

verses.text: Have mercy on me, O God, according to Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}; according to Your great compassion, blot out my transgressions.
Wash me clean of my iniquity and cleanse me from my sin.
For I know my transgressions, and my sin is always before me.
Against You, You only, have I sinned and done what is evil in Your sight, so that You may be proved right when You speak and blameless when You judge.
Surely I was brought forth in iniquity; I was sinful when my mother conceived me.
Surely You desire truth in the inmost being; You teach me wisdom in the inmost place.

units.label: Create in me a clean heart, O God
units.note: the central prayer for cleansing and renewal

verses.text: Purify me with hyssop, and I will be clean; wash me, and I will be whiter than snow.
Let me hear joy and gladness; let the bones You have crushed rejoice.
Hide Your face from my sins and blot out all my iniquities.
{{bara|Create}} in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right {{ruach|spirit}} within me.
Cast me not away from Your presence; take not Your Holy Spirit from me.
Restore to me the joy of Your salvation, and sustain me with a willing spirit.

units.label: Then I will teach transgressors Your ways
units.note: the vow to praise and teach

verses.text: Then I will teach transgressors Your ways, and sinners will return to You.

Deliver me from bloodguilt, O God, the God of my salvation, 
and my tongue will sing of Your righteousness.
O Lord, open my lips, and my mouth will declare Your praise.
For You do not delight in sacrifice, or I would bring it; You take no pleasure in burnt offerings.

The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and a contrite heart, 
O God, You will not despise.

units.label: Build up the walls of Jerusalem

verses.text: In Your good pleasure, cause Zion to prosper; build up the walls of Jerusalem.

Then You will delight in righteous sacrifices, in whole burnt offerings; 
then bulls will be offered on Your altar.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion. David's opening plea rests entirely on this quality of God's character, not on any merit of his own, appealing to the same loving devotion that grounds every promise of mercy in Scripture.

bara.gloss: To create, specifically the verb used in Genesis 1:1 for God's creation from nothing. David does not ask God merely to repair or improve his heart but to bara, to create something wholly new, as radical as the first creation itself.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. David prays for a right ruach within him and pleads that God's Holy Spirit not be taken from him, language that anticipates the Spirit given permanently to God's people under the new covenant.

basic.context: This is David's prayer after Nathan the prophet confronted him over his adultery with Bathsheba and his arrangement of her husband Uriah's death, 2 Samuel 11 to 12. David does not make excuses. He confesses his sin as against God above all, admits that sin runs all the way down to his very nature, and pleads not merely for forgiveness but for an entirely new heart and a right spirit within him.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm is meant to be prayed, not just studied. It is widely regarded as the model of true repentance in Scripture: honest, undefended confession that moves beyond regret over consequences to sorrow over sin against God, and beyond mere pardon to a longing for inner transformation.
basic.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's chesed, His covenant loving devotion, as the sole ground of mercy. His central plea, bara, create in me a clean heart, uses the same verb for God's creation from nothing in Genesis 1:1, and he begs that God's ruach, His Holy Spirit, not be withdrawn from him.
basic.typology: The hyssop and cleansing David describes point forward to the true cleansing accomplished by Christ's blood. His plea for a new heart and a right spirit anticipates the new covenant promise of a new heart and God's own Spirit placed within His people.
basic.christ: The cleansing David seeks with hyssop, verse 7, foreshadows the true cleansing of conscience accomplished by the blood of Christ, Hebrews 9:13-14, 1 John 1:7. His plea, create in me a clean heart and renew a right spirit, anticipates the new covenant promise of a new heart and God's Spirit within His people, Ezekiel 36:26-27, fulfilled in the new birth Jesus describes in John 3:3-8. David's bloodguilt, incurred through Uriah's murder, is answered ultimately by the innocent blood of Christ shed for the guilty.
basic.god: God is both perfectly just, so that His judgment is right even when it falls on His own anointed king, and abundantly merciful, ready to create a clean heart and restore the joy of salvation to the truly repentant. He does not delight in ritual sacrifice divorced from a broken and contrite heart.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 12:1-13 records the historical events behind this psalm, Nathan's confrontation and David's confession. Ezekiel 36:26-27 promises a new heart and God's Spirit within His people. Hebrews 9:13-14 shows Christ's blood cleansing the conscience far beyond any Old Testament ritual.
basic.oneStory: David's honest confession and plea for a new heart is the model of repentance for every sinner. That plea is answered fully in Christ, whose blood cleanses what hyssop only pictured, and whose Spirit creates the clean heart David could only pray for, poured out on all who trust Him under the new covenant.

context.literary: One of the penitential psalms, moving from urgent plea, verses 1-2, through confession, verses 3-6, to a central prayer for renewal, verses 7-12, a vow of praise and teaching, verses 13-17, and a closing prayer for Zion, verses 18-19, likely a later liturgical addition applying David's personal repentance to the whole community.
context.historical: The heading fixes this psalm precisely: written after Nathan the prophet came to David following his adultery with Bathsheba and the murder of her husband Uriah, 2 Samuel 11-12, one of the darkest episodes in David's reign and one of the most honest responses to sin in all of Scripture.
context.canonical: Stands as the definitive Old Testament model of repentance, echoed in the tax collector's prayer in Luke 18:13 and in the New Testament's call to confess sin honestly, 1 John 1:9, while its plea for a new heart anticipates the new covenant promises fulfilled in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confess sin honestly before God without excuse, to plead for both forgiveness and inner transformation, and to model for every future reader what true, God-centered repentance looks and sounds like.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own historical repentance after a specific sin; it prescribes the pattern every believer is to follow when confessing sin, honest, undefended, seeking not only pardon but a changed heart.

debates.question: Does David's plea in verse 11, 'take not Your Holy Spirit from me,' suggest a believer can lose salvation?

views.label: It reflects Old Testament experience, not a New Testament doctrine of losing salvation
views.case: In the Old Testament the Spirit came upon specific individuals, often for particular tasks, and could be withdrawn, as happened to Saul, 1 Samuel 16:14; David rightly fears this experiential loss without this teaching anything about the permanent indwelling promised under the new covenant.

views.label: It expresses the proper posture of dependence every believer should still pray, without implying salvation is easily lost
views.case: Even under the new covenant, believers are told not to grieve the Holy Spirit, Ephesians 4:30, and David's prayer models a healthy, ongoing dependence on God's sustaining grace rather than presumption, whatever the precise mechanics of assurance.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the verse expresses urgent, humble dependence on God's grace rather than self-confident presumption, a posture every believer under any covenant should share.

study.originalLanguages: David grounds his entire plea in God's chesed, covenant loving devotion, not his own merit. His central request, bara, create in me a clean heart, uses the very verb for God's creation from nothing, and he pleads that God's ruach, Spirit, would not be withdrawn but would renew and sustain him.

typology.fulfillment: The true cleansing of the conscience accomplished by the blood of Christ, Hebrews 9:13-14, 1 John 1:7.

typology.type: The plea for a new, clean heart and a right spirit, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant promise of a new heart and God's own Spirit within His people, Ezekiel 36:26-27, fulfilled in the new birth Jesus describes in John 3.

study.christ: David's cry for cleansing with hyssop, verse 7, foreshadows the true and final cleansing of conscience accomplished by the blood of Christ, Hebrews 9:13-14, 1 John 1:7. His central plea, create in me a clean heart and renew a right spirit, anticipates the new covenant promise of a new heart and God's own Spirit placed within His people, Ezekiel 36:26-27, fulfilled in the new birth Jesus describes to Nicodemus, John 3:3-8. David's bloodguilt from Uriah's murder finds its answer in the innocent blood of Christ, shed once for the guilty.
study.god: God is perfectly just, His judgment right even against His own anointed king, and abundantly merciful, willing to create a clean heart and restore the joy of salvation to the one who comes honestly. He values a broken and contrite heart far above the outward form of sacrifice.

crossReferences.note: The historical account behind this psalm, Nathan's confrontation and David's confession.
The new covenant promise of a new heart and God's Spirit placed within His people.
Christ's blood cleanses the conscience far beyond what any Old Testament ritual could accomplish.

study.oneStory: David's honest confession and his plea for a new heart stand as the model of repentance for every sinner. That plea is answered fully in Christ, whose blood cleanses what hyssop could only picture, and whose Spirit creates the clean heart David could only pray for, poured out on all who trust Him under the new covenant.

sermon.title: A Broken and Contrite Heart God Will Not Despise
sermon.why: Piper walks through David's downward spiral into sin and his four responses of repentance in this psalm, showing how Christ's atonement grounds the outrageous mercy God shows David, and how every believer today lays hold of that same mercy by turning to Christ, praying for cleansing, confessing the depth of sin, and pleading for renewal.

quiz.q: What is the historical occasion of this psalm, according to its heading?

options.text: David wrote it after Nathan the prophet confronted him over his adultery with Bathsheba and the death of Uriah.
options.note: This is stated directly in the psalm's heading and matches the events of 2 Samuel 11-12.

options.text: David wrote it while hiding from Saul in a cave.
options.note: That setting belongs to other psalms, such as Psalm 57, not this one.

options.text: David wrote it after his victory over Goliath.
options.note: This psalm is a penitential prayer over grave sin, not a victory song.

options.text: David wrote it during his coronation as king.
options.note: The psalm's entire tone is one of confession and repentance, not celebration of enthronement.

quiz.q: In verse 4, why does David say his sin is 'against You, You only'?

options.text: Because what makes sin truly sinful is that it is ultimately an offense against God, even though it deeply harmed others too.
options.note: This does not deny the real harm to Bathsheba, Uriah, and others; it identifies the deepest dimension of sin as an offense against God.

options.text: Because Bathsheba and Uriah were not actually harmed by his actions.
options.note: This directly contradicts the historical record; both were gravely harmed, and Uriah was killed.

options.text: Because human sin can never truly affect other people, only God.
options.note: Scripture consistently teaches sin harms others; the verse is about sin's deepest nature, not a denial of human harm.

options.text: Because David believed no human authority had the right to judge a king.
options.note: The point of the verse is theological, about the nature of sin itself, not a claim about royal immunity from human judgment.

quiz.q: What is David's central request in verse 10, and why is it significant?

options.text: Create in me a clean heart, using the same Hebrew verb, bara, as God's creation from nothing in Genesis 1:1.
options.note: This shows David is not asking for mere reform but for an entirely new creation within him.

options.text: Give me a new house to live in.
options.note: The request is for inner spiritual renewal, not material provision.

options.text: Restore my kingdom and my throne.
options.note: The psalm's concern is inner cleansing and renewal, not political restoration.

options.text: Teach me to avoid future temptation through willpower alone.
options.note: David asks God to create something new in him, not merely to strengthen his own willpower.

quiz.q: According to verses 16-17, what does God ultimately desire more than sacrifice?

options.text: A broken spirit and a contrite heart.
options.note: Verse 17 states this explicitly as what God will not despise.

options.text: Larger and costlier sacrifices than before.
options.note: The text says the opposite; God does not delight in sacrifice or burnt offerings for their own sake here.

options.text: Public displays of royal wealth.
options.note: This is not mentioned; the emphasis is entirely on inward brokenness before God.

options.text: A vow of silence.
options.note: David in fact vows to teach and sing praise, not to remain silent; the emphasis is on a broken and contrite heart.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's plea for a clean heart find its fulfillment in the New Testament?

options.text: In the new covenant promise of a new heart and God's own Spirit within His people, fulfilled in the new birth Jesus describes in John 3.
options.note: Ezekiel 36:26-27 and John 3:3-8 directly answer David's plea for a clean heart and a right spirit.

options.text: In the teaching that no one can ever truly be made clean before God.
options.note: This contradicts the entire thrust of the gospel, that Christ's blood does cleanse believers.

options.text: In the abolition of the need for repentance altogether.
options.note: The New Testament continues to call believers to ongoing confession and repentance, 1 John 1:9.

options.text: In a purely future hope with no present experience of renewal.
options.note: Scripture teaches believers experience real, present renewal now through the Spirit, not merely a future hope.

questions: David confesses honestly without excuse, appealing only to God's steadfast love. Where do you tend to minimize, excuse, or hide your sin rather than confessing it as plainly as David does here?
David asks not merely for forgiveness but for God to create in him a clean heart. Are you seeking only relief from guilt, or are you also asking God to change the desires beneath your sin?

Notably, David never asks God for more willpower against temptation; he asks for the joy of God's salvation to be restored. How might chasing joy in God, rather than mere self-control, change the way you fight a particular temptation?
David says God does not delight in sacrifice divorced from a broken and contrite heart. Where might you be relying on religious activity to cover over an unrepentant heart?
David resolves that once restored, he will teach transgressors and sinners will return to God. How might your own history of sin and grace equip you to help someone else who is struggling right now?

translationNotes.note: Brought forth in iniquity does not excuse David's specific sin by blaming his birth; rather, it intensifies his confession, showing that his adultery and murder were fruit of a deeper, inborn corruption rather than isolated, uncharacteristic acts.
Hyssop was the branch priests used to sprinkle blood for ceremonial cleansing, Leviticus 14:4-7; David appeals to God as his true priest to declare him clean.
Create renders bara, the same verb used only of God's activity in Genesis 1:1, implying David asks not for repair or improvement but for something entirely new.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 51:1-19. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of desiringgod.org, confirming the scripture tag Psalm 51 and the sermon date June 8, 2008.
